1.synthesis:
Leviticus 20 is the enforcement chapter of the Holiness Code: where chapter 18 listed the forbidden sexual and religious practices, chapter 20 specifies the penalties. The chapter opens not with sexual violations but with Molech-worship (vv. 2–5), deliberately placing the worst violation first. Giving one's children to Molech — child sacrifice for divine favour — is capital: the people of the land are commanded to stone the offender, and if they fail to do so (vv. 4–5), YHWH himself will act with the karet penalty against both the offender and his family. The community bears collective responsibility for enforcing covenant holiness; passive complicity in the face of Molech-worship is itself a covenantal failure.

The rationale is given in v. 3: Molech-worship defiles YHWH's sanctuary and profanes his holy name. This connects the chapter's structure to the holiness of the sanctuary established in chapters 1–16 and the holiness of the community in chapters 17–19. The three defilements are inseparable: the sexual ethics of chapter 18, the social ethics of chapter 19, and the sanctuary-purity of the earlier chapters all belong to one integrated system. To violate any one of these is to strike at all of them.

Consulting mediums and necromancers (v. 6) receives karet — cutting off from the people — rather than capital punishment. The language is that of spiritual adultery: 'whoring after' (liznot achareihem) the dead. YHWH's response — 'I will set my face against that person' — is the language of direct divine judgment. The section closes with the holiness call of vv. 7–8: 'Consecrate yourselves, therefore, and be holy, for I am YHWH your God. Keep my statutes and do them; I am YHWH who sanctifies you.' The chapter embeds its penalty-schedule within the positive call to holiness: the penalties exist not as ends in themselves but as the enforcement system that protects covenant community life.
1.historical_context:
The chapter structure — prohibition chapter followed by penalty chapter — is a recognisable ancient Near Eastern legal pattern (paralleled in Hittite law codes). The Holiness Code adapts this format but grounds its penalty-logic differently: the penalties are not social deterrents alone but expressions of covenant seriousness. The capital penalty for Molech-worship reflects the gravity of child sacrifice in the ancient context: it was not merely murder but a religious act that directly competed with YHWH's own claim on human children as his image-bearers. The community-stoning (ragom) for Molech-worship involves the whole people in the enforcement of covenant holiness — collective action preventing collective defilement.

The extended triple formula in v. 5 — YHWH will set his face against the man, his family, and all who whore with him — signals that Molech-worship is systemic, not merely individual. The family unit participates in the religious practices of its head; the penalty therefore encompasses the household.
1.literary_structure: The chapter is structured around escalating penalties: child sacrifice opens with death-by-stoning (v. 2); medium-consulting receives karet (v. 6); the sexual offenses of vv. 9–21 range from death through karet through childlessness, in a deliberate hierarchy. The chapter's opening (Molech) and closing (vv. 22–27) frame the sexual penalties between two theological poles: the worst religious violation and the call to holiness.
1.theological_application: The inclusion of the community in enforcing the Molech prohibition — and the penalty for passive complicity — teaches a permanent principle: covenant communities bear collective responsibility for the moral environment they tolerate. A church that publicly sanctions what the covenant prohibits, or that silently tolerates known covenant violations without pastoral response, participates in the defilement. This does not mean the church wields the civil penalties of the Holiness Code (which belonged to Israel as a theocratic nation), but it does mean that church discipline — the new covenant's version of community enforcement — is a covenant obligation, not merely an optional pastoral tool.
1.christology: The anti-Molech chapter finds its Christological antithesis in the cross: where Molech demanded the offering of children to secure divine favour, the Father offered his own Son to secure the redemption of children. Romans 8:32 makes the structural contrast explicit: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all.' The divine face-setting against Molech-worshipers in v. 3 becomes, in the gospel, the divine face-turning toward the penitent in Christ — the wrath that the text describes is absorbed by the one who sanctifies the community (v. 8: 'I am YHWH who sanctifies you').

9.synthesis:
The longest section of Leviticus 20 is the penalty schedule for the sexual violations catalogued in chapter 18. The list opens with the capital penalty for cursing parents (v. 9) — a family violation placed at the head of the sexual violations, signalling that the family order the sexual laws protect begins at the highest level of parental authority. The penalties then move through adultery (v. 10), a man and his father's wife (v. 11), a man and his daughter-in-law (v. 12), male same-sex intercourse (v. 13), a man marrying both a woman and her mother (v. 14), bestiality (vv. 15–16), and a series of violations carrying karet or 'childlessness' rather than death.

The gradation of penalties is theologically significant. The most severe violations — those that most directly attack the fundamental structure of covenant kinship (stepmother, daughter-in-law, mother-and-daughter combination, bestiality) — receive death. Same-sex intercourse (v. 13) is placed in the death-penalty category and described as toevah, the same category-violation term used in the prohibition of Leviticus 18:22. Sister-incest (v. 17) receives karet with the additional designation chesed (disgrace), suggesting that it is treated as a covenant shame rather than a capital crime. Intercourse during menstruation (v. 18) receives karet for both parties. Aunt-nephew and uncle-wife unions (vv. 19–20) receive 'childlessness' (arirím), a penalty that is more natural consequence than judicial sentence.

The formula 'their blood is upon them' (damam bam, occurring in v. 9,11,12,13,16) is the chapter's refrain of moral responsibility: the penalty falls on the offenders themselves, not on the community that enforces it. The community is not guilty of the death it executes; the guilt falls on those whose act required the penalty.
9.historical_context:
The equal penalty for adultery in v. 10 — both the adulterer and the adulteress die — is remarkable in an ancient Near Eastern context where most legal codes penalised the woman far more severely or exclusively. The Holiness Code insists on equal culpability for both parties in sexual covenant violations, reflecting its consistent concern for equal dignity of persons. The encounter in John 8:3–11 — where only the woman is brought to Jesus, with no mention of her partner — is a Pharisaic violation of precisely this principle: they invoked the law against the woman while exempting the man.

The 'childlessness' penalty of vv. 20–21 is the text's most natural-consequence penalty: unions within certain prohibited degrees produce either literal infertility or covenantal childlessness — children born of such unions have no legitimate inheritance in Israel's covenant community.
9.literary_structure: The section is structured by penalty-severity: capital offenses (vv. 9–16), karet offenses (vv. 17–18), and childlessness penalties (vv. 19–21). Within each tier, the violations move from the most to the less severe within the same category. The formula 'their blood is upon them' creates a regular refrain that punctuates the capital-penalty list, maintaining a tone of solemnity rather than harshness.
9.theological_application: The death penalties of Leviticus 20 are not directly enforceable in the church, which is not a theocratic civil community. But the theology they encode — that sexual covenant violations are serious, that they carry real consequences, and that community accountability is required — is permanently valid. The church enforces the equivalent through discipline: the expulsion from the covenant community of the unrepentant sexual offender is the new-covenant analogue to the karet penalty (cf. 1 Corinthians 5:1–13, where Paul demands the karet-equivalent for the incest case). The goal of discipline, as in Leviticus, is the holiness of the community — but in the new covenant it also carries the possibility of restoration through repentance.
9.christology: The adultery case in John 8:3–11 is the most direct NT engagement with the Leviticus 20 penalty schedule. Jesus does not abolish the law ('let him who is without sin among you be the first to throw a stone') but enforces the principle that the accuser must themselves be without the violation they prosecute. His response — 'neither do I condemn you; go, and from now on sin no more' — is not an abolition of the moral standard but a demonstration that the one who alone could legitimately enforce the law chooses, in the current age of grace, to extend mercy while calling to repentance. The moral standard stands; the penalty is absorbed by the one who bore the world's sin.

22.synthesis:
The closing section of Leviticus 20 returns to the theological framework established in chapter 18: the land as moral agent, Israel's covenant status as YHWH's separated people, and the positive vocation of holiness. Verse 22 reopens with the land-vomiting warning: 'You shall therefore keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them, that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out (velo taqii etchem ha'arets).' The penalty chapter has listed the consequences of covenant violation at the legal level; here the consequences at the cosmic level are named: the land itself expels its inhabitants when they defile it.

The promise that follows in v. 24 is one of the Holiness Code's most direct election-statements: 'I am YHWH your God, who has separated you from the peoples (hifdal etchem min-ha'ammim).' The separation is not ethic superiority but covenant-vocation: YHWH's clean/unclean distinctions in food law (v. 25) are the daily enacted symbol of Israel's distinctive status. The food-law rationale here is explicitly theological — 'you shall distinguish between the clean animal and the unclean' — because the distinction between Israel and the nations is itself a living parable of holiness: some things are set apart for YHWH, others are not.

The chapter's climax is in v. 26: 'You shall be holy to me, for I YHWH am holy and have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine (lihyot li).' Lihyot li — 'to be mine' — is the final cause of the entire Holiness Code. Every law in chapters 17–20 serves this ultimate purpose: YHWH wants a people who are his. The sexual ethics, the social ethics, the food laws, the penalty schedule — all are instruments of the same goal: a community that embodies the character of the God who owns them. The chapter closes with the capital penalty for mediums and necromancers (v. 27), a final bookend with the opening Molech-prohibition: the chapter that began by condemning the greatest apostasy ends by condemning the most pervasive occult practice.
22.historical_context:
The food-law rationale in vv. 25–26 links the dietary distinctions of Leviticus 11 to the election-theology of chapters 17–20. The daily practice of choosing between clean and unclean food is the embodied rehearsal of Israel's covenant status — they are people who make YHWH's distinctions their own. The abrogation of food laws in the New Testament (Acts 10:9–16; Mark 7:14–19) does not abolish the underlying theology of election-separation but transfers it from dietary practice to the inclusive gathering of all nations into the covenant community — the vision of Isaiah 56:6–7.

The formula lihyot li ('to be mine') appears in the patriarchal covenant (Genesis 17:8), in the Sinai covenant preamble (Exodus 19:5), and here at the climax of the Holiness Code — it is the covenant's constant refrain, unchanged across every phase of redemptive history.
22.literary_structure: Verses 22–27 mirror the structure of the closing section of chapter 18: both end with the land-vomiting warning, both include the ger (implied in ch18, the food distinctions in ch20), and both conclude with a direct statement of YHWH's separating purpose. This structural echo binds chapters 18–20 together as a single legislative unit: prohibition (ch18), positive ethics (ch19), penalties (ch20).
22.theological_application: The lihyot li ('to be mine') formula is the permanent theological goal of the entire Holiness Code. The church exists not to maintain moral order for its own sake but to be YHWH's own possession — a people who reflect his character in the world. Every ethical standard the NT upholds from the Holiness Code serves this same purpose: the community of the redeemed is to be visibly distinctive, not to achieve salvation, but because they already belong to the God who is holy. Holiness is the shape of belonging, not the price of entry.
22.christology: The lihyot li formula reaches its fullest expression in Christ's high-priestly prayer: 'they are yours. All mine are yours, and yours are mine, and I am glorified in them' (John 17:9–10). The Father's ownership of the covenant people, expressed in the Holiness Code's election-language, is confirmed and deepened through the Son's mediation: the church belongs to YHWH through Christ, and Christ's own holiness is the ground of the community's holy calling. 1 Peter 2:9 quotes the election-language of Exodus 19:5–6 and extends it to the Gentile church — the separation from the nations in Leviticus 20 becomes, in the new covenant, the gathering of the nations into the one community that belongs to God.
